AI.motivation: GFlowNets have proven powerful for applications like molecule and material discovery, but their training dynamics remain difficult to interpret. Standard ML tools track metrics but don't reveal how models explore sample spaces, construct trajectories, or shift sampling probabilities during training.
AI.method: Developed GFlowState visual analytics system with multiple coordinated views: candidate rankings chart, state projection, node-link diagram of trajectory network, and transition heatmap. These visualizations enable analysis of sampling trajectories, comparison with reference datasets, and training dynamics investigation.
AI.result: Case studies demonstrate the system effectively supports debugging and assessing GFlowNet quality across application domains. The visualizations enable users to investigate sampling behavior, policy evolution, identify underexplored regions, and find sources of training failure.
AI.conclusion: By making the structural dynamics of GFlowNets observable, GFlowState enhances their interpretability and can accelerate GFlowNet development in practice. The system bridges the gap between powerful probabilistic frameworks and practical debugging/training analysis needs.

title: A Scale-Adaptive Framework for Joint Spatiotemporal Super-Resolution with Diffusion Models
summary: Deep-learning video super-resolution has progressed rapidly, but climate applications typically super-resolve (increase resolution) either space or time, and joint spatiotemporal models are often designed for a single pair of super-resolution (SR) factors (upscaling spatial and temporal ratio between the low-resolution sequence and the high-resolution sequence), limiting transfer across spatial resolutions and temporal cadences (frame rates). We present a scale-adaptive framework that reuses the same architecture across factors by decomposing spatiotemporal SR into a deterministic prediction of the conditional mean, with attention, and a residual conditional diffusion model, with an optional mass-conservation (same precipitation amount in inputs and outputs) transform to preserve aggregated totals. Assuming that larger SR factors primarily increase underdetermination (hence required context and residual uncertainty) rather than changing the conditional-mean structure, scale adaptivity is achieved by retuning three factor-dependent hyperparameters before retraining: the diffusion noise schedule amplitude beta (larger for larger factors to increase diversity), the temporal context length L (set to maintain comparable attention horizons across cadences) and optionally a third, the mass-conservation function f (tapered to limit the amplification of extremes for large factors). Demonstrated on reanalysis precipitation over France (Comephore), the same architecture spans super-resolution factors from 1 to 25 in space and 1 to 6 in time, yielding a reusable architecture and tuning recipe for joint spatiotemporal super-resolution across scales.

AI.tldr: A scale-adaptive framework for joint spatiotemporal video super-resolution that reuses the same architecture across different upscaling factors by combining deterministic attention-based prediction with residual conditional diffusion modeling.
AI.motivation: Current deep-learning video super-resolution methods for climate applications typically super-resolve either space OR time, not both jointly. Existing joint models are designed for single pairs of SR factors, limiting transferability across different spatial resolutions and temporal cadences.
AI.method: The framework decomposes spatiotemporal SR into: 1) deterministic prediction of conditional mean with attention, and 2) residual conditional diffusion model. It achieves scale adaptivity by retuning three factor-dependent hyperparameters: diffusion noise schedule amplitude (β), temporal context length (L), and optionally a mass-conservation function. This allows the same architecture to work across different SR factors.
AI.result: The same architecture successfully spans super-resolution factors from 1 to 25 in space and 1 to 6 in time, demonstrated on reanalysis precipitation data over France (Comephore). This yields a reusable architecture and tuning recipe for joint spatiotemporal super-resolution across scales.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a practical scale-adaptive framework that enables joint spatiotemporal super-resolution across multiple scales using a single reusable architecture, addressing limitations of previous methods that were constrained to specific SR factor pairs.

title: Low-Rank Adaptation Redux for Large Models
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) has emerged as the de facto standard for parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) of foundation models, enabling the adaptation of billion-parameter networks with minimal computational and memory overhead. Despite its empirical success and rapid proliferation of variants, it remains elusive which architectural choices, optimization techniques, and deployment constraints should guide practical method selection. This overview revisits LoRA through the lens of signal processing (SP), bridging modern adapter designs with classical low-rank modeling tools and inverse problems, as well as highlighting how SP principles can inform principled advances of fine-tuning approaches. Rather than providing a comprehensive enumeration and empirical comparisons of LoRA variants, emphasis is placed on the technical mechanisms underpinning these approaches to justify their effectiveness. These advances are categorized into three complementary axes: architectural design, efficient optimization, and pertinent applications. The first axis builds on singular value decomposition (SVD)-based factorization, rank-augmentation constructions, and cross-layer tensorization, while the second axis deals with initialization, alternating solvers, gauge-invariant optimization, and parameterization-aware methods. Beyond fine-tuning, emerging applications of LoRA are accounted across the entire lifecycle of large models, ranging from pre- and post-training to serving/deployment. Finally, open research directions are outlined at the confluence of SP and deep learning to catalyze a bidirectional frontier: classical SP tools provide a principled vocabulary for designing principled PEFT methods, while the unique challenges facing modern deep learning, especially the overwhelming scale and prohibitive overhead, also offer new research lines benefiting the SP community in return.

AI.tldr: A systematic review of Low-rank Adaptation (LoRA) methods through a signal processing lens, analyzing architectural design, optimization techniques, and applications across the large model lifecycle.
AI.motivation: Despite LoRA's popularity as a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method, there's a lack of principled guidance on architectural choices, optimization techniques, and deployment constraints. The paper aims to bridge modern adapter designs with classical signal processing tools to provide theoretical foundations.
AI.method: The paper revisits LoRA through a signal processing perspective, categorizing advances into three axes: 1) architectural design (SVD-based factorization, rank-augmentation, cross-layer tensorization), 2) efficient optimization (initialization, alternating solvers, gauge-invariant optimization), and 3) applications across model lifecycle.
AI.result: The analysis reveals how signal processing principles can inform principled advances in fine-tuning approaches, moving beyond empirical comparisons to justify effectiveness through technical mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a bidirectional research frontier where classical signal processing tools provide vocabulary for designing principled PEFT methods, while modern deep learning challenges offer new research opportunities for the SP community, outlining open research directions at this intersection.

title: The Sample Complexity of Multicalibration
summary:
We study the minimax sample complexity of multicalibration in the batch setting. A learner observes $n$ i.i.d. samples from an unknown distribution and must output a (possibly randomized) predictor whose population multicalibration error, measured by Expected Calibration Error (ECE), is at most $\varepsilon$ with respect to a given family of groups. For every fixed $κ> 0$, in the regime $|G|\le \varepsilon^{-κ}$, we prove that $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-3})$ samples are necessary and sufficient, up to polylogarithmic factors. The lower bound holds even for randomized predictors, and the upper bound is realized by a randomized predictor obtained via an online-to-batch reduction. This separates the sample complexity of multicalibration from that of marginal calibration, which scales as $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-2})$, and shows that mean-ECE multicalibration is as difficult in the batch setting as it is in the online setting, in contrast to marginal calibration which is strictly more difficult in the online setting. In contrast we observe that for $κ= 0$, the sample complexity of multicalibration remains $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-2})$ exhibiting a sharp threshold phenomenon.
  More generally, we establish matching upper and lower bounds, up to polylogarithmic factors, for a weighted $L_p$ multicalibration metric for all $1 \le p \le 2$, with optimal exponent $3/p$. We also extend the lower-bound template to a regular class of elicitable properties, and combine it with the online upper bounds of Hu et al. (2025) to obtain matching bounds for calibrating properties including expectiles and bounded-density quantiles.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes optimal sample complexity bounds for multicalibration in batch learning, showing Θ̃(ε⁻³) samples are necessary and sufficient when |𝒢| ≤ ε⁻ᵏ for fixed κ>0, separating it from marginal calibration (Θ̃(ε⁻²)).
AI.motivation: There's a gap in understanding the sample complexity requirements for multicalibration compared to marginal calibration. While marginal calibration has known bounds, the sample complexity of multicalibration with respect to Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and its relationship to online settings remained unclear.
AI.method: The study uses minimax analysis to prove matching upper and lower bounds for multicalibration sample complexity. They employ information-theoretic lower bounds for randomized predictors and achieve upper bounds via an online-to-batch reduction. The analysis extends to weighted Lₚ multicalibration metrics and general elicitable properties.
AI.result: For fixed κ>0 and |𝒢| ≤ ε⁻ᵏ, multicalibration requires Θ̃(ε⁻³) samples (optimal exponent 3/p for Lₚ metrics), separating it from marginal calibration (Θ̃(ε⁻²)). The lower bounds hold even for randomized predictors, while upper bounds are achieved via randomized predictors from online-to-batch conversion. A sharp threshold occurs at κ=0 where complexity drops to Θ̃(ε⁻²).
AI.conclusion: Multicalibration with ECE is significantly harder than marginal calibration in batch settings (cubic vs quadratic dependence), matching online setting difficulty. The results provide a complete picture of minimax sample complexity for multicalibration across different group complexities and extend to broader calibration properties.

title: Fine-Tuning Regimes Define Distinct Continual Learning Problems
summary: Continual learning (CL) studies how models acquire tasks sequentially while retaining previously learned knowledge. Despite substantial progress in benchmarking CL methods, comparative evaluations typically keep the fine-tuning regime fixed. In this paper, we argue that the fine-tuning regime, defined by the trainable parameter subspace, is itself a key evaluation variable. We formalize adaptation regimes as projected optimization over fixed trainable subspaces, showing that changing the trainable depth alters the effective update signal through which both current task fitting and knowledge preservation operate. This analysis motivates the hypothesis that method comparisons need not be invariant across regimes. We test this hypothesis in task incremental CL, five trainable depth regimes, and four standard methods: online EWC, LwF, SI, and GEM. Across five benchmark datasets, namely MNIST, Fashion MNIST, KMNIST, QMNIST, and CIFAR-100, and across 11 task orders per dataset, we find that the relative ranking of methods is not consistently preserved across regimes. We further show that deeper adaptation regimes are associated with larger update magnitudes, higher forgetting, and a stronger relationship between the two. These results show that comparative conclusions in CL can depend strongly on the chosen fine-tuning regime, motivating regime-aware evaluation protocols that treat trainable depth as an explicit experimental factor.

AI.tldr: CL method rankings change depending on which model parameters are fine-tuned; deeper tuning causes more forgetting but better task fitting, requiring explicit consideration of trainable depth in evaluations.
AI.motivation: Current CL evaluations typically keep the fine-tuning regime (trainable parameters) fixed, but the authors argue this regime is a key variable that should be explicitly studied since it affects both current task learning and knowledge preservation.
AI.method: Formalized adaptation regimes as projected optimization over fixed trainable subspaces. Tested the hypothesis in task incremental CL using five trainable depth regimes (different parameter subsets) and four standard methods (online EWC, LwF, SI, GEM) across five benchmark datasets (MNIST, Fashion MNIST, KMNIST, QMNIST, CIFAR-100) with 11 task orders per dataset.
AI.result: Relative rankings of CL methods are not consistently preserved across different trainable depth regimes. Deeper adaptation regimes lead to larger update magnitudes, higher forgetting, and a stronger relationship between update magnitude and forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Comparative conclusions in CL strongly depend on the chosen fine-tuning regime, necessitating regime-aware evaluation protocols that treat trainable depth as an explicit experimental factor rather than keeping it fixed.

title: Temporal Taskification in Streaming Continual Learning: A Source of Evaluation Instability
summary: Streaming Continual Learning (CL) typically converts a continuous stream into a sequence of discrete tasks through temporal partitioning. We argue that this temporal taskification step is not a neutral preprocessing choice, but a structural component of evaluation: different valid splits of the same stream can induce different CL regimes and therefore different benchmark conclusions. To study this effect, we introduce a taskification-level framework based on plasticity and stability profiles, a profile distance between taskifications, and Boundary-Profile Sensitivity (BPS), which diagnoses how strongly small boundary perturbations alter the induced regime before any CL model is trained. We evaluate continual finetuning, Experience Replay, Elastic Weight Consolidation, and Learning without Forgetting on network traffic forecasting with CESNET-Timeseries24, keeping the stream, model, and training budget fixed while varying only the temporal taskification. Across 9-, 30-, and 44-day splits, we observe substantial changes in forecasting error, forgetting, and backward transfer, showing that taskification alone can materially affect CL evaluation. We further find that shorter taskifications induce noisier distribution-level patterns, larger structural distances, and higher BPS, indicating greater sensitivity to boundary perturbations. These results show that benchmark conclusions in streaming CL depend not only on the learner and the data stream, but also on how that stream is taskified, motivating temporal taskification as a first-class evaluation variable.

AI.tldr: Temporal taskification (how continuous streams are split into discrete tasks) significantly affects streaming continual learning evaluation outcomes, not just the learner or data stream itself.
AI.motivation: Current streaming continual learning approaches convert continuous streams into discrete tasks via temporal partitioning, but this taskification step is treated as neutral preprocessing rather than a structural evaluation component that can influence benchmark conclusions.
AI.method: Introduced a taskification-level framework with plasticity/stability profiles, profile distance metrics, and Boundary-Profile Sensitivity (BPS) to measure how boundary perturbations alter regimes. Evaluated continual finetuning, Experience Replay, EWC, and LwF on network traffic forecasting with CESNET-Timeseries24, keeping stream/model/training fixed while varying temporal taskification across 9-, 30-, and 44-day splits.
AI.result: Substantial changes in forecasting error, forgetting, and backward transfer across different taskifications. Shorter taskifications induced noisier distribution patterns, larger structural distances, and higher BPS (greater sensitivity to boundary perturbations). Taskification alone can materially affect CL evaluation outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark conclusions in streaming CL depend not only on the learner and data stream, but also on how the stream is taskified, motivating temporal taskification as a first-class evaluation variable rather than a neutral preprocessing choice.

title: Handbook of Rough Set Extensions and Uncertainty Models
comment: 159 pages. Peer-Reviewed Book. ISBN: 978-1-59973-867-3. Publisher: Neutrosophic Science International Association (NSIA) Publishing House
summary: Rough set theory models uncertainty by approximating target concepts through lower and upper sets induced by indiscernibility, or more generally, by granulation relations in data tables. This perspective captures vagueness caused by limited observational resolution and supports set-theoretic reasoning about what can be determined with certainty and what remains only possible. This book is written as a map of models. Rather than developing a single algorithmic pipeline in depth, it provides a systematic survey of the main rough set paradigms and their extension routes. More specifically, representative variants are organized according to (i) the underlying granulation mechanism, such as equivalence-based, tolerance-based, covering-based, neighborhood-based, and probabilistic approximations, and (ii) the uncertainty semantics attached to data and relations, such as crisp, fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, and plithogenic settings. The book also explains how each choice changes the form of approximations and the interpretation of boundary regions. Throughout the book, small illustrative examples are used to clarify modeling intent and typical use cases in classification and decision support. Finally, an important clarification of scope should be noted. Since the main purpose of this book is to provide a map of models, the Abstract and Introduction should not lead readers to expect that feature reduction and rule induction are primary objectives. Although these topics are central in the rough set literature, they are treated here mainly as motivating applications and as entry points to the broader research landscape. The principal aim of the book is to survey and position rough set models and their extensions in a systematic and coherent manner.

AI.tldr: A survey book providing a systematic map of rough set theory models and their extensions, organized by granulation mechanisms and uncertainty semantics rather than focusing on algorithmic pipelines like feature reduction.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive survey and positioning of rough set models and their extensions in a systematic and coherent manner, rather than developing specific algorithms or focusing primarily on applications like feature reduction and rule induction.
AI.method: Organizes rough set variants according to (i) granulation mechanisms (equivalence-based, tolerance-based, covering-based, neighborhood-based, probabilistic) and (ii) uncertainty semantics (crisp, fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, plithogenic settings). Uses illustrative examples to clarify modeling intent and use cases.
AI.result: A systematic map of rough set paradigms that explains how different choices of granulation mechanisms and uncertainty semantics affect approximations and boundary region interpretations, with examples illustrating classification and decision support applications.
AI.conclusion: This book serves as a comprehensive reference map for understanding rough set theory's diverse modeling approaches, positioning various extensions in relation to each other while clarifying that its primary focus is on model survey rather than algorithmic development.
